nativeRange.habitat: Generally found on shallow coral reefs and rocky substrates across the tropical Indo-Pacific, with the aquarium trade sourcing animals primarily from the Marshall Islands. Occupies abandoned gastropod (e.g. snail or triton) shells and shelters in rockwork and crevices between foraging bouts.

decorPreferences: live rock with abundant caves and crevices for hiding
sand or fine rubble substrate for sifting and burrowing
an assortment of empty gastropod shells of progressively larger sizes
mature reef tank with stable parameters
secure rockwork (animals will dislodge unstable pieces while foraging)

diet.notes: The Electric Blue Hermit Crab is an opportunistic omnivore. In the aquarium it generally accepts nuisance algae such as green hair algae and cyanobacteria, detritus, leftover fish food, dried seaweed or Nori, frozen mysis or brine shrimp, and small pieces of raw shrimp or fish. In tanks with abundant algae growth, supplemental feeding once or twice a week is commonly suggested. When available algae has been depleted, more regular offerings of meaty food are advisable, as a well-fed crab is less likely to prey on tank snails and tubeworms.

goodWith: peaceful reef fish (gobies, blennies, cardinalfish, small wrasses)
most LPS, SPS, soft corals, and zoanthids
other small reef-safe hermits and shrimp of similar size
larger snails with shells the crab cannot fit into
avoidWith: small or thin-shelled snails (commonly killed for their shells)
feather duster worms and small tubeworms (have been reported eaten)
very small ornamental shrimp and tiny inverts (predation risk when dense)
predatory wrasses, triggerfish, large puffers, and large hawkfish

imageCandidates.notes: Fafner, 1844x1844. Tack-sharp, well-lit whole animal showing the diagnostic electric-blue-and-black banded legs, blue eyestalks and bright orange antennae; best overall.
User:Haplochromis, 1018x676. Clear aquarium view of the whole crab, banded blue/black legs and orange antennae well shown; slightly soft through glass.
Daniel Eisenhauer, 2048x1375. In-situ wild tidepool shot, whole animal sharp and vibrant with banded legs, blue eyestalks and orange antennae; adds natural-habitat variety.

summary: The Electric Blue Hermit Crab is an Indo-Pacific ornamental hermit with vivid blue-and-black banded legs and bright orange antennae. It is widely kept as a cleanup-crew member, grazing hair algae and cyanobacteria in reef aquariums.
careNotes: The Electric Blue Hermit Crab (Calcinus elegans) is a small-to-mid-sized marine hermit in the family Diogenidae, native to the tropical Indo-Pacific and most commonly collected from the Marshall Islands. The legs are alternately banded electric blue and black, the eyestalks are blue, and the antennae and mouthparts are bright orange, making it one of the more visually distinctive ornamental hermits available to reef keepers. It is a useful cleanup-crew species that grazes filamentous green hair algae and cyanobacteria and sifts the sand bed for detritus, contributing to substrate aeration. Suitable water conditions are 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, specific gravity 1.023–1.025, and dKH 8–12, in line with standard reef aquarium parameters. The species will not damage corals, but it is well-documented as predatory toward small snails — often killing them to take their shells — and toward small tubeworms, and may pick at other small sessile invertebrates in densely stocked tanks. Providing an assortment of progressively larger empty gastropod shells is important: it reduces shell-related predation on snails and allows the crab to move into a larger shell as it grows between molts. The species tends to be peaceful toward fish and most invertebrates of similar or larger size, but individuals can squabble with other hermit crabs over shells, particularly in smaller tanks where suitable shells are scarce. A minimum tank size of around 75 L (approximately 20 US gallons) is commonly recommended. Like all crustaceans, the Electric Blue Hermit Crab is sensitive to copper-based medications; any treatment containing copper must not be used in a tank where the species is kept.
breedingNotes: The Electric Blue Hermit Crab has not been bred in captivity in any documented case, and the trade is entirely wild-caught from the Indo-Pacific. Like most marine hermits, Calcinus elegans has a planktonic larval phase — the newly hatched young, called zoea, must drift in open water — which makes rearing them in a typical home aquarium impractical.

crustacean.moltingNotes: A typical molt interval has not been established for this species. Adequate iodine, calcium, and trace elements in the water are commonly cited as important for proper molting. The shed exoskeleton (the molt) is generally left in the tank to be consumed, as this helps the crab reclaim calcium. In the hours immediately after molting, the crab is soft and vulnerable to predation by tankmates and to shell theft by other hermits.
crustacean.breedingNotes: No reliably reported captive-breeding success has been documented for this species. The planktonic larval phase makes rearing in a home display tank impractical.
